Zoox Continues Expanding Robotaxi Services
data.description:
By Connor Hart


Zoox will expand its robotaxi service into new U.S. markets and grow operations in existing cities as it continues to scale its footprint.

The Amazon.com-owned company said Tuesday that it plans to launch its robotaxi service in Austin, Texas, and Miami, Florida, where it has been testing its technology since mid-2024.

"The initial rollout will be within a small area of both cities, and rides will first be available to Zoox employees, their families, and friends," the company said, noting that operating areas will expand as testing progresses.

The company also plans to increase operations in San Francisco and Las Vegas, where it currently operates.

Zoox said it will quadruple its operating area in San Francisco this spring, extending service across much of the eastern half of the city. In Las Vegas, the company plans to more than double its service footprint, adding destinations including the Las Vegas Convention Center and major hotels along the Strip, as well as venues such as Sphere and T-Mobile Arena.

Chief Executive Aicha Evans said the expansion reflects progress made through earlier deployments.

"We are actively implementing learnings to confidently and safely scale our robotaxi service across the country and bring our differentiated experience to even more riders," she said.


Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-24-26 1102ET

data.title: Goldman Sachs' Struyven on raising 2026 Brent Forecast
data.description:
Daan Struyven, Co-Head of Global Commodities Research at Goldman Sachs (GS), outlined the firm's revised outlook for Brent crude, raising its forecast to an average of $85 per barrel this year, up from the previous estimate of $77. He noted that the disruption in the Strait of Hormuz represents the largest supply shock ever seen in the global crude market. He speaks with Shery Ahn and Haidi-Stroud Watts on "Bloomberg: The Asia Trade".

Richmond Fed's District Saw Flat Manufacturing This Month
data.description:
By Matt Grossman

Manufacturing activity in the Richmond Fed's region was flat this month, an improvement from a contraction in February, the reserve bank's monthly survey showed.

The survey's composite index improved from minus 10 in February - indicating a decline in activity - to 0 in March. The reserve bank's district covers the coastal states from Maryland to South Carolina, as well as most of West Virginia.

The survey's hiring index remained in contraction territory, as it has been for most of the past year.

Meanwhile, firms continued to report elevated price pressures.

Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

U.S. Business Activity Growth Slowed in March
data.description:
By Jessica Coacci

Activity in the U.S. private sector expanded at a slower pace this month, a monthly survey said Tuesday, as businesses reported a slightly weaker upturn in new orders and a spike in prices following the outbreak of the war in the Middle East.

The S&P Global Flash U.S. Composite PMI--based on a survey of around 650 manufacturers and 500 service providers-fell to 51.4 this month from 51.9 in February. A reading above 50 indicates activity continued to grow.

"Companies are reporting a hit to demand from the additional uncertainty and cost of living impact generated by the conflict," said Chris Williamson, chief business economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence.

The service sector was harder hit as manufacturers reported an upturn in output and new order book growth, the survey said.

Employment fell for the first time in more than a year as firms generally sought to reduce overhead costs in the uncertain economic climate. Meanwhile, average input costs rose at a sharp rate again, posting the largest monthly increase for 10 months and feeding through to the largest increase in average selling prices since August 2022.

"Companies are meanwhile building safety stocks amid concerns that the war may lead to more protracted supply issues and price rises while trimming headcounts to reduce overheads," the survey said.

Write to Jessica Coacci at jessica.coacci@wsj.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires
